# The Taste Gym

> A gym for musical taste. Instruments that measure David Hume's five criteria of taste
> ("Of the Standard of Taste", 1757) as numbers, using performance tasks where the user can be
> wrong — never self-report, never personality prediction. Free assessment; scores are honest:
> results are labeled provisional until real cohort norms exist, and no percentile is ever
> fabricated.

The flagship instrument is the Prestige Test: rate eight music clips blind, then rate the same
clips with artist names attached — three labels are deliberately swapped (and disclosed in a
mandatory debrief). The blind-vs-labeled rating gap is a measured prestige-bias number; the user
serves as their own control. All scoring is deterministic code — no AI classifies the user.

## Core pages

- [The Taste Gym (home)](/): what the gym is; the visible machines, open and locked
- [The Prestige Test](/bias): the flagship instrument — takes about four minutes, free, no sign-up
- [Reading room](/learn): all explainers — the five criteria, the instruments, the methodology
- [What is the Prestige Test?](/learn/prestige-bias-test): the design, the sanctioned deception, the debrief
- [Methodology](/learn/methodology): performance tasks, within-subject control, deterministic scoring, honest-norms policy

## Hume's five criteria (one explainer each)

- [Delicacy](/learn/delicacy): the key-in-the-wine story; the planned Delicacy Trials (controlled audio degradations with objectively right answers)
- [Practice](/learn/practice): why taste is trainable; retests and progression
- [Comparison](/learn/comparison): Ogilby vs. Milton; breadth as measurable exposure
- [Freedom from prejudice](/learn/freedom-from-prejudice): the criterion the Prestige Test measures
- [Good sense](/learn/good-sense): calibration — confidence vs. accuracy, Brier scores

## Optional

- [llms-full.txt](/llms-full.txt): the full product and methodology description in one file
